patterns.explanation: You're missing a semicolon (;) or comma (,). Every statement in C must end with a semicolon. If you're declaring multiple variables, separate them with commas. Check the line just above where the error occurred.
You're missing a closing parenthesis ')'. Make sure all opening parentheses have matching closing ones.
You're missing a closing brace '}' somewhere in your code. Count your opening '{' and closing '}' braces - they should match!
You need to specify the data type (like int, float, char) for your variable. In C, every variable must have a type declaration.
You're using '{}' without declaring it first. Either you forgot to declare this variable, misspelled it, or forgot to include the necessary header file.

You're calling function '{}' but haven't declared it. If it's a standard library function (like printf, scanf), include the appropriate header file (#include <stdio.h>). If it's your own function, declare it before using it.
You've declared '{}' with different types in different places. Make sure all declarations of this function or variable use the same type throughout your program.
You've defined '{}' more than once. Each function or variable can only be defined once in the same scope. Remove the duplicate definition.
You're trying to declare '{}' again in the same scope. Once a variable is declared, you can't declare it again - you can only use it or assign new values to it.
You're calling function '{}' without enough arguments. Check the function definition to see how many parameters it needs and provide them all.
You're passing too many arguments to function '{}'. Check the function definition to see exactly how many parameters it expects.

patterns.regex: control reaches end of non-void function
patterns.explanation: Your function should return a value, but there's a path through the code where it doesn't. Add a return statement at the end of your function.

patterns.regex: return-statement with no value, in function returning
patterns.explanation: Your function should return a value, but you used 'return;' without a value. Change it to 'return <value>;' with an appropriate value.

patterns.regex: void value not ignored as it ought to be
patterns.explanation: You're trying to use the return value of a function that doesn't return anything (void function). Void functions can't be used in expressions or assignments.
You're trying to assign a pointer of one type to a pointer of a different type. Make sure both sides of the assignment have matching pointer types, or use proper type casting.

patterns.regex: subscripted value is not an array, pointer, or vector
patterns.explanation: You're trying to use array notation [] on something that isn't an array or pointer. Check that you're using the correct variable and that it's actually declared as an array.

patterns.regex: array subscript is not an integer
patterns.explanation: Array indices must be integers. You're trying to access an array element using a non-integer value (like a float or char). Convert it to an integer first.
The compiler expected an expression but found something else instead. This often happens when you have mismatched parentheses or forget to put a value in an expression.
There's an unexpected character '{}' in your code. This might be a special character that accidentally got inserted, or you might be using the wrong type of quote marks.
The compiler doesn't recognize the type '{}'. Either you misspelled it, forgot to define it (for custom types), or need to include a header file that defines it.
The compiler can't determine how much memory to allocate for '{}'. This usually happens with incomplete type definitions or missing header files.
Argument {} of function '{}' has the wrong type. Check what type the function expects and make sure you're passing the correct type of value.

patterns.regex: lvalue required as left operand of assignment
patterns.explanation: You can only assign values to variables, not to expressions or constants. The left side of '=' must be a variable or memory location that can be modified.
You're initializing a pointer with another pointer of an incompatible type. Make sure the pointer types match, or use proper casting if necessary.
You declared variable '{}' but never used it. This is just a warning - your code will still compile. Consider removing unused variables to keep your code clean.

patterns.regex: format ['"]?(.+?)['"]? expects argument of type ['"]?(.+?)['"]?, but argument (\d+) has type
patterns.explanation: In your printf/scanf, the format specifier '{}' expects type '{}', but you're passing a different type in position {}. Use the correct format specifier for your data type (e.g., %d for int, %f for float, %c for char).

patterns.regex: passing argument (\d+) of ['"]?(.+?)['"]? makes pointer from integer without a cast
patterns.explanation: In argument {} of function '{}', you're passing an integer where a pointer is expected. You might have forgotten the '&' operator to get the address of a variable.

patterns.regex: passing argument (\d+) of ['"]?(.+?)['"]? makes integer from pointer without a cast
patterns.explanation: In argument {} of function '{}', you're passing a pointer where an integer is expected. You might need to dereference the pointer using the '*' operator.
The compiler expected a variable name or opening parenthesis but found something else. Check your function declarations and variable names for syntax errors.

patterns.regex: break statement not within loop or switch
patterns.explanation: You can only use 'break' inside loops (for, while) or switch statements. It looks like your 'break' is outside of these structures.

patterns.regex: continue statement not within a loop
patterns.explanation: You can only use 'continue' inside loops (for, while, do-while). It looks like your 'continue' is outside of a loop.

patterns.regex: case label not within a switch statement
patterns.explanation: You can only use 'case' labels inside a switch statement. Make sure your case is properly nested within a switch block.
